abstract:
Autonomous agents have long stood as a cornerstone of research in both academia and industry. Historically, agent development has relied on training systems within confined environments using restricted knowledge bases, a methodology that differs sharply from the expansive, open-ended way humans learn. This gap has made the achievement of human-level decision-making a persistent challenge. However, the emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs)—trained on massive scales of web knowledge—has demonstrated a remarkable capacity for human-like intelligence, sparking a transformative wave of research into LLM-based autonomous agents. 

This paper provides a systematic and comprehensive survey of this burgeoning field. We offer a holistic review by first proposing a unified conceptual framework for the construction of LLM-based agents, categorized into modules for profiling, memory, planning, and action. Grounded in this framework, we analyze how agents acquire specialized capabilities through various fine-tuning and mechanism-based strategies. Furthermore, we present an extensive overview of the diverse applications of these agents across the social sciences, natural sciences, and engineering. Finally, we examine contemporary evaluation methodologies, encompassing both subjective human assessments and objective benchmarks. By synthesizing the current state of the art, we identify critical open challenges and outline promising future directions for the development of next-generation autonomous agents.
